title: Gharana

infobox.value: | | Melody | | --- | | * [Vocals](/wiki/Singing "Singing") * [Sitar](/wiki/Sitar "Sitar") * [Sarod](/wiki/Sarod "Sarod") * [Surbahar](/wiki/Surbahar "Surbahar") * [Rudra veena](/wiki/Rudra_veena "Rudra veena") * [Violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") * [Sarangi](/wiki/Sarangi "Sarangi") * [Esraj/Dilruba](/wiki/Esraj "Esraj") * [Bansuri](/wiki/Bansuri "Bansuri") * [Shehnai](/wiki/Shehnai "Shehnai") * [Santoor](/wiki/Santoor "Santoor") * [Harmonium](/wiki/Pump_organ#Harmonium "Pump organ") * [Jal tarang](/wiki/Jal_tarang "Jal tarang") | | Rhythm | | * [Tabla](/wiki/Tabla "Tabla") * [Pakhawaj](/wiki/Pakhavaj "Pakhavaj") | | Drone | | * [Tanpura](/wiki/Tanpura "Tanpura") * [Shruti box](/wiki/Shruti_box "Shruti box") * [Swarmandal](/wiki/Swarmandal "Swarmandal") |
Founding Artists | Approximate founding date | Famous Exponents | Features

**[Abu'l Hasan Yamīn ud-Dīn Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau")** & His 12 Students | 13th Century | [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") Mir Bula Kalawant, Mir Hassan Sawant, Mir Gunwant, Shah Shah Sadarang, Shah Adarang, SheikhFiroz (Shah Bhikan) Taj Khan  (Rang Rus) Sheikh Ajmal (Miyan Achpal) Mir Qutub Baksh (Miyan Tanras) Haji Muhammed Siddiq Khan, Muzaffar Khan | The **Qawwal Bacchon Ka Gharana** is the oldest [khayal](/wiki/Khayal "Khayal") gharana of the [Hindustani Classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_music "Hindustani Classical music") tradition.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Members of this gharana approach raagdari with more freedom than the dhrupad-informed gharanas, like [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana"), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur-Atrauli_gharana "Jaipur-Atrauli gharana"), and [Agra](/wiki/Agra_gharana "Agra gharana").[[6]](#cite_note-6)  Emphasis on bhav and exposition are the hallmarks of this style.[[3]](#cite_note-CI-3)  Repertoire[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Repertoire")]  In addition to extensive khayal compositions, the gharana is known for its [qawwals](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Hazrat Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Hazrat_Amir_Khusrau "Hazrat Amir Khusrau"), Miyan Samti | 13th century | [Sadarang](/wiki/Sadarang "Sadarang"), [Adarang](/wiki/Adarang "Adarang"), Miyan Achpal, [Tanras Khan](/wiki/Tanras_Khan "Tanras Khan"), [Mamman Khan](/wiki/Mamman_Khan "Mamman Khan"), Chand Khan, Nasir Ahmed Khan, Krishna Bisht, [Iqbal Ahmad Khan](/wiki/Iqbal_Ahmad_Khan "Iqbal Ahmad Khan") | Oldest Khayal gharana, wide range of taans, bol baant, bol taan, fast taan pattern, gamak taan, emphasis on melody and laykari, structured badhat of raga

Nathan Pir Baksh, Hassu Khan, Haddu Khan, [Nathu Khan](/wiki/Nathu_Khan "Nathu Khan") | Mid 16th Century | [Vishnu Digambar Paluskar](/wiki/Vishnu_Digambar_Paluskar "Vishnu Digambar Paluskar"), [Omkarnath Thakur](/wiki/Omkarnath_Thakur "Omkarnath Thakur"), [Ghulam Hassan Shaggan](/wiki/Ghulam_Hassan_Shaggan "Ghulam Hassan Shaggan"), [D. V. Paluskar](/wiki/D._V._Paluskar "D. V. Paluskar"), [Pt Raja Bhaiya Poochwale](/wiki/Raja_Bhaiya_Poonchwale "Raja Bhaiya Poonchwale"), [Pt. Balasaheb Poochwale](/wiki/Balasaheb_Poonchwale "Balasaheb Poonchwale"), [Sunanda Patnaik](/wiki/Sunanda_Patnaik "Sunanda Patnaik"), [Malini Rajurkar](/wiki/Malini_Rajurkar "Malini Rajurkar"), [Veena Sahasrabuddhe](/wiki/Veena_Sahasrabuddhe "Veena Sahasrabuddhe"), [N Rajam](/wiki/N_Rajam "N Rajam") | The most noticeable trait of this gharana is strong emphasis on gamaks in taans, as well as use of Bol-baant, bol-taan, no use of sargam, wide range in taans, alankarik taans, descending sapaat taans, roughly similar emphasis on melody and rhythm, preference for simple (as opposed to compound) ragas, repertoire of bandishes, variety of taans

Ghagge Khudabaksh | Mid-19th century | [Faiyaz Khan](/wiki/Faiyaz_Khan "Faiyaz Khan"), [Vilayat Hussain Khan](/wiki/Vilayat_Hussain_Khan "Vilayat Hussain Khan"), [Sharafat Hussain Khan](/wiki/Sharafat_Hussain_Khan "Sharafat Hussain Khan"), [Shrikrishna Ratanjankar](/w/index.php?title=Shrikrishna_Ratanjankar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shrikrishna Ratanjankar (page does not exist)"), [Jitendra Abhisheki](/wiki/Jitendra_Abhisheki "Jitendra Abhisheki") | Closer to dhrupad with nom-tom type alap and other elements, rhythmic play, frequent use of tisra jati in teentaal, emphasis on voice culture to achieve wide range and powerful throw of voice, bol-baant, bol-taan, rare use of sargam, slower taans, use of jabda taan, repertoire of traditional and self-composed bandishes

[Abdul Karim Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Karim_Khan "Abdul Karim Khan"), [Abdul Wahid Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Wahid_Khan "Abdul Wahid Khan") | Late 19th century | ["Proudh Gandharva" Pandit Vishwanathbuwa Jadhav](/wiki/Vishwanath_Jadhav "Vishwanath Jadhav"), Pandit B. V. Jadhav, Pandit R. V. Jadhav, Shakuntlaraje R. Jadhav, Pandit P. V. Jadhav [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi"), [Prabha Atre](/wiki/Prabha_Atre "Prabha Atre"), [Hirabai Barodekar](/wiki/Hirabai_Barodekar "Hirabai Barodekar"), [Gangubai Hangal](/wiki/Gangubai_Hangal "Gangubai Hangal")  [Amir Khan](/wiki/Amir_Khan_(singer) "Amir Khan (singer)") (Highly Influenced from Kirana Gharana) | Kirana is often considered to be the flag-bearer of Hindustani Classical vocal music. Foremost intention of this gharana is perfect intonation of notes and emphasis on melody, Also, Slow-tempo raga development, long and sustained pitches, usually traditional ragas, use of sargam, very little bol-baant, clarity of text pronunciation, use of some Carnatic ragas and raga features, emphasis on vocal as opposed to instrumental form. Highly decorative and complex taans.

Chhajju Khan, Nazeer Khan, Khadim Hussain Khan | Late 19th century | [Aman Ali Khan](/wiki/Aman_Ali_Khan "Aman Ali Khan"), [Anjanibai Malpekar](/wiki/Anjanibai_Malpekar "Anjanibai Malpekar"), [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar") [Amir Khan](/wiki/Amir_Khan_(singer) "Amir Khan (singer)") (Highly Influenced from Bhendibazar Gharana) | Emphasis on breath control to be able to sing long passages in a single breath, use of merukhand for extended alaps, use of gamak taan and sargam, use of some Carnatic ragas

[Alladiya Khan](/wiki/Alladiya_Khan "Alladiya Khan") | Late 19th century | [Kesarbai Kerkar](/wiki/Kesarbai_Kerkar "Kesarbai Kerkar"), [Mogubai Kurdikar](/wiki/Mogubai_Kurdikar "Mogubai Kurdikar"), [Mallikarjun Mansur](/wiki/Mallikarjun_Mansur "Mallikarjun Mansur"), [Kishori Amonkar](/wiki/Kishori_Amonkar "Kishori Amonkar"), [Ashwini Bhide-Deshpande](/wiki/Ashwini_Bhide-Deshpande "Ashwini Bhide-Deshpande") | Repertoire of rare and complex ragas, based on Agra gharana, use of aakaar for badhat, heavy use of teentaal, rupak, jhaptaal and ada-chautaal, rhythmic play, use of bol-baant and bol-taan, rippling taans, heavy emphasis on taans

& His 12 Students | 13th Century | [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") Mir Bula Kalawant, Mir Hassan Sawant, Mir Gunwant, Shah Shah Sadarang, Shah Adarang, SheikhFiroz (Shah Bhikan) Taj Khan  (Rang Rus) Sheikh Ajmal (Miyan Achpal) Mir Qutub Baksh (Miyan Tanras) Haji Muhammed Siddiq Khan, Muzaffar Khan | The **Qawwal Bacchon Ka Gharana** is the oldest [khayal](/wiki/Khayal "Khayal") gharana of the [Hindustani Classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_music "Hindustani Classical music") tradition.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Members of this gharana approach raagdari with more freedom than the dhrupad-informed gharanas, like [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana"), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur-Atrauli_gharana "Jaipur-Atrauli gharana"), and [Agra](/wiki/Agra_gharana "Agra gharana").[[6]](#cite_note-6)  Emphasis on bhav and exposition are the hallmarks of this style.[[3]](#cite_note-CI-3)  Repertoire[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Repertoire")]  In addition to extensive khayal compositions, the gharana is known for its [qawwals](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali").[[7]](#cite_note-7) |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Kadambari_playing_sitar%2C_edit.jpg/250px-Kadambari_playing_sitar%2C_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kadambari_playing_sitar,_edit.jpg)

*Kadambari playing sitar* by [Raja Raja Varma](/wiki/Raja_Raja_Varma "Raja Raja Varma")

In [Hindustani music](/wiki/Hindustani_music "Hindustani music") (North Indian classical music), a ***gharānā*** is a system of social organisation in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), linking musicians or dancers by lineage or apprenticeship, and more importantly by adherence to a particular musical style.

The word gharana comes from the Hindi word 'ghar' which means 'house'. It typically refers to the place where the musical ideology originated; for example, some of the gharanas well known for singing [khyals](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal") are: Gwalior, Delhi, Agra, Indore, Kashmiri, Atrauli-Jaipur, Kirana and Patiala.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Four famous [kathak](/wiki/Kathak "Kathak") gharanas are: Lucknow, Atrauli-Jaipur, Benares and Raigarh.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Vocal gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Vocal gharanas")]

See also: [Category:Vocal gharanas](/wiki/Category:Vocal_gharanas "Category:Vocal gharanas")

### Khyal gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Khyal gharanas")]

The gharana system in khyal was rooted in the *[guru–shishya tradition](/wiki/Guru%E2%80%93shishya_tradition "Guru–shishya tradition")* and was similar to the [Dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") *Bani* system. The gharana system was greatly influenced by the gradual fall of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), which forced musicians to move from Delhi to princely states such as Gwalior, Lucknow, Hyderabad, Patiala and Rampur.

The gharanas have distinct styles of presenting the [khyal](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal") — how much to emphasize and how to enunciate the words of the composition, when to sing the *sthayi* and *antara*, whether to sing an unmetered *alap* in the beginning, what kinds of improvisations to use, how much importance to give to the rhythmic aspect, and so on. However, an individual performer from a gharana may choose to borrow appealing stylistic aspects of another gharana in his or her *gayaki* (singing style). There are ten prominent khyal gharanas, and they are:[[3]](#cite_note-CI-3)[[4]](#cite_note-itc-4)

| Gharana | Founding Artists | Approximate founding date | Famous Exponents | Features |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Qawwal Bacchon ka gharana](/wiki/Qawwal_Bacchon_gharana "Qawwal Bacchon gharana") | **[Abu'l Hasan Yamīn ud-Dīn Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau")** & His 12 Students | 13th Century | [Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Amir_Khusrau "Amir Khusrau") Mir Bula Kalawant, Mir Hassan Sawant, Mir Gunwant, Shah Shah Sadarang, Shah Adarang, SheikhFiroz (Shah Bhikan) Taj Khan  (Rang Rus) Sheikh Ajmal (Miyan Achpal) Mir Qutub Baksh (Miyan Tanras) Haji Muhammed Siddiq Khan, Muzaffar Khan | The **Qawwal Bacchon Ka Gharana** is the oldest [khayal](/wiki/Khayal "Khayal") gharana of the [Hindustani Classical music](/wiki/Hindustani_Classical_music "Hindustani Classical music") tradition.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Members of this gharana approach raagdari with more freedom than the dhrupad-informed gharanas, like [Gwalior](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana"), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur-Atrauli_gharana "Jaipur-Atrauli gharana"), and [Agra](/wiki/Agra_gharana "Agra gharana").[[6]](#cite_note-6)  Emphasis on bhav and exposition are the hallmarks of this style.[[3]](#cite_note-CI-3)  Repertoire[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Repertoire")]  In addition to extensive khayal compositions, the gharana is known for its [qawwals](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali").[[7]](#cite_note-7) |
| [Dilli gharana](/wiki/Delhi_gharana "Delhi gharana") | [Hazrat Amir Khusrau](/wiki/Hazrat_Amir_Khusrau "Hazrat Amir Khusrau"), Miyan Samti | 13th century | [Sadarang](/wiki/Sadarang "Sadarang"), [Adarang](/wiki/Adarang "Adarang"), Miyan Achpal, [Tanras Khan](/wiki/Tanras_Khan "Tanras Khan"), [Mamman Khan](/wiki/Mamman_Khan "Mamman Khan"), Chand Khan, Nasir Ahmed Khan, Krishna Bisht, [Iqbal Ahmad Khan](/wiki/Iqbal_Ahmad_Khan "Iqbal Ahmad Khan") | Oldest Khayal gharana, wide range of taans, bol baant, bol taan, fast taan pattern, gamak taan, emphasis on melody and laykari, structured badhat of raga |
| [Gwalior gharana](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana") | Nathan Pir Baksh, Hassu Khan, Haddu Khan, [Nathu Khan](/wiki/Nathu_Khan "Nathu Khan") | Mid 16th Century | [Vishnu Digambar Paluskar](/wiki/Vishnu_Digambar_Paluskar "Vishnu Digambar Paluskar"), [Omkarnath Thakur](/wiki/Omkarnath_Thakur "Omkarnath Thakur"), [Ghulam Hassan Shaggan](/wiki/Ghulam_Hassan_Shaggan "Ghulam Hassan Shaggan"), [D. V. Paluskar](/wiki/D._V._Paluskar "D. V. Paluskar"), [Pt Raja Bhaiya Poochwale](/wiki/Raja_Bhaiya_Poonchwale "Raja Bhaiya Poonchwale"), [Pt. Balasaheb Poochwale](/wiki/Balasaheb_Poonchwale "Balasaheb Poonchwale"), [Sunanda Patnaik](/wiki/Sunanda_Patnaik "Sunanda Patnaik"), [Malini Rajurkar](/wiki/Malini_Rajurkar "Malini Rajurkar"), [Veena Sahasrabuddhe](/wiki/Veena_Sahasrabuddhe "Veena Sahasrabuddhe"), [N Rajam](/wiki/N_Rajam "N Rajam") | The most noticeable trait of this gharana is strong emphasis on gamaks in taans, as well as use of Bol-baant, bol-taan, no use of sargam, wide range in taans, alankarik taans, descending sapaat taans, roughly similar emphasis on melody and rhythm, preference for simple (as opposed to compound) ragas, repertoire of bandishes, variety of taans |
| [Agra gharana](/wiki/Agra_gharana "Agra gharana") | Ghagge Khudabaksh | Mid-19th century | [Faiyaz Khan](/wiki/Faiyaz_Khan "Faiyaz Khan"), [Vilayat Hussain Khan](/wiki/Vilayat_Hussain_Khan "Vilayat Hussain Khan"), [Sharafat Hussain Khan](/wiki/Sharafat_Hussain_Khan "Sharafat Hussain Khan"), [Shrikrishna Ratanjankar](/w/index.php?title=Shrikrishna_Ratanjankar&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shrikrishna Ratanjankar (page does not exist)"), [Jitendra Abhisheki](/wiki/Jitendra_Abhisheki "Jitendra Abhisheki") | Closer to dhrupad with nom-tom type alap and other elements, rhythmic play, frequent use of tisra jati in teentaal, emphasis on voice culture to achieve wide range and powerful throw of voice, bol-baant, bol-taan, rare use of sargam, slower taans, use of jabda taan, repertoire of traditional and self-composed bandishes |
| [Kirana gharana](/wiki/Kirana_gharana "Kirana gharana") | [Abdul Karim Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Karim_Khan "Abdul Karim Khan"), [Abdul Wahid Khan](/wiki/Abdul_Wahid_Khan "Abdul Wahid Khan") | Late 19th century | ["Proudh Gandharva" Pandit Vishwanathbuwa Jadhav](/wiki/Vishwanath_Jadhav "Vishwanath Jadhav"), Pandit B. V. Jadhav, Pandit R. V. Jadhav, Shakuntlaraje R. Jadhav, Pandit P. V. Jadhav [Sawai Gandharva](/wiki/Sawai_Gandharva "Sawai Gandharva"), [Bhimsen Joshi](/wiki/Bhimsen_Joshi "Bhimsen Joshi"), [Prabha Atre](/wiki/Prabha_Atre "Prabha Atre"), [Hirabai Barodekar](/wiki/Hirabai_Barodekar "Hirabai Barodekar"), [Gangubai Hangal](/wiki/Gangubai_Hangal "Gangubai Hangal")  [Amir Khan](/wiki/Amir_Khan_(singer) "Amir Khan (singer)") (Highly Influenced from Kirana Gharana) | Kirana is often considered to be the flag-bearer of Hindustani Classical vocal music. Foremost intention of this gharana is perfect intonation of notes and emphasis on melody, Also, Slow-tempo raga development, long and sustained pitches, usually traditional ragas, use of sargam, very little bol-baant, clarity of text pronunciation, use of some Carnatic ragas and raga features, emphasis on vocal as opposed to instrumental form. Highly decorative and complex taans. |
| [Bhendibazaar gharana](/wiki/Bhendibazaar_gharana "Bhendibazaar gharana") | Chhajju Khan, Nazeer Khan, Khadim Hussain Khan | Late 19th century | [Aman Ali Khan](/wiki/Aman_Ali_Khan "Aman Ali Khan"), [Anjanibai Malpekar](/wiki/Anjanibai_Malpekar "Anjanibai Malpekar"), [Lata Mangeshkar](/wiki/Lata_Mangeshkar "Lata Mangeshkar") [Amir Khan](/wiki/Amir_Khan_(singer) "Amir Khan (singer)") (Highly Influenced from Bhendibazar Gharana) | Emphasis on breath control to be able to sing long passages in a single breath, use of merukhand for extended alaps, use of gamak taan and sargam, use of some Carnatic ragas |
| [Jaipur-Atrauli gharana](/wiki/Jaipur-Atrauli_gharana "Jaipur-Atrauli gharana") | [Alladiya Khan](/wiki/Alladiya_Khan "Alladiya Khan") | Late 19th century | [Kesarbai Kerkar](/wiki/Kesarbai_Kerkar "Kesarbai Kerkar"), [Mogubai Kurdikar](/wiki/Mogubai_Kurdikar "Mogubai Kurdikar"), [Mallikarjun Mansur](/wiki/Mallikarjun_Mansur "Mallikarjun Mansur"), [Kishori Amonkar](/wiki/Kishori_Amonkar "Kishori Amonkar"), [Ashwini Bhide-Deshpande](/wiki/Ashwini_Bhide-Deshpande "Ashwini Bhide-Deshpande") | Repertoire of rare and complex ragas, based on Agra gharana, use of aakaar for badhat, heavy use of teentaal, rupak, jhaptaal and ada-chautaal, rhythmic play, use of bol-baant and bol-taan, rippling taans, heavy emphasis on taans |
| [Patiala gharana](/wiki/Patiala_gharana "Patiala gharana") | [Bade Fateh Ali Khan](/wiki/Bade_Fateh_Ali_Khan "Bade Fateh Ali Khan"), [Ali Baksh Khan](/wiki/Ali_Baksh "Ali Baksh") | Late 19th century | [Bade Ghulam Ali Khan](/wiki/Bade_Ghulam_Ali_Khan "Bade Ghulam Ali Khan"), [Fateh Ali](/wiki/Bade_Fateh_Ali_Khan "Bade Fateh Ali Khan")-[Amanat Ali Khan](/wiki/Amanat_Ali_Khan "Amanat Ali Khan"), [Vasantrao Deshpande](/wiki/Vasantrao_Deshpande "Vasantrao Deshpande"), [Jagdish Prasad](/wiki/Jagdish_Prasad "Jagdish Prasad"), [Sandhya Mukherjee](/wiki/Sandhya_Mukherjee "Sandhya Mukherjee"), Prasun Banerjee, Meera Banerjee, [Ajoy Chakrabarty](/wiki/Ajoy_Chakrabarty "Ajoy Chakrabarty") | Patiala is often considered to be the amalgamate of few pre-existing gharanas and has claimed to combine the musical traditions of [Delhi gharana](/wiki/Delhi_gharana "Delhi gharana"), [Gwalior gharana](/wiki/Gwalior_gharana "Gwalior gharana") and [Jaipur-Atrauli Gharana](/wiki/Jaipur-Atrauli_Gharana "Jaipur-Atrauli Gharana") and is known for borrowing elements from other gharanas. This Gharana is also known for its versatility, not only known for its Khayal singing but also for venturing into other forms of classical music and placing a strong foothold there as well. Emphasis on voice development, roughly similar emphasis on melody and rhythm, bol-baant-like sargam with occasional tonic transpositions, occasional use of bol-taan, variety of taans, fast sargam and taan patterns, may or may not include antara, influence of [tappa](/wiki/Tappa "Tappa") style |
| [Rampur-Sahaswan gharana](/wiki/Rampur-Sahaswan_gharana "Rampur-Sahaswan gharana") | [Inayat Hussain Khan](/wiki/Inayat_Hussain_Khan "Inayat Hussain Khan") | Mid 19th century | [Mushtaq Hussain Khan](/wiki/Mushtaq_Hussain_Khan "Mushtaq Hussain Khan"), [Shanno Khurana](/wiki/Shanno_Khurana "Shanno Khurana"), [Rashid Khan (musician)](/wiki/Rashid_Khan_(musician) "Rashid Khan (musician)"), [Nissar Hussain Khan](/wiki/Nissar_Hussain_Khan "Nissar Hussain Khan"), [Ghulam Mustafa Khan (singer)](/wiki/Ghulam_Mustafa_Khan_(singer) "Ghulam Mustafa Khan (singer)"), [Ghulam Sadiq Khan](/wiki/Ghulam_Sadiq_Khan "Ghulam Sadiq Khan"), Ghulam Abbas Khan | Emphasis on melody, bol-taans, sargam taans, highly varied aakar taans and good hold over laykari, along with strong command over [Tarana](/wiki/Tarana "Tarana") |
| [Indore gharana](/wiki/Indore_gharana "Indore gharana") | [Amir Khan](/wiki/Amir_Khan_(singer) "Amir Khan (singer)") | Mid 20th century |  | Slow-tempo and leisurely raga development, improvisation mostly in lower and middle octaves, tendency towards serious and expansive ragas, emphasis on melody, judicious use of pause between improvisations, bol alap and sargam using merukhand patterns, sparing application of [murki](/wiki/Murki "Murki"), use of kan swaras in all parts of performance, controlled use of embellishments to preserve introspective quality, rare use of tihai, careful enunciation of text, may or may not include antara, multiple laya jatis in a single taan, mixture of taan types in a single taan, known for ruba'idar [Tarana](/wiki/Tarana "Tarana") (considered similar to chhota khyal) |
| [Mewati gharana](/wiki/Mewati_gharana "Mewati gharana") | [Ghagge Nazir Khan](/wiki/Ghagge_Nazir_Khan "Ghagge Nazir Khan") | Mid 19th century | [Jasraj](/wiki/Jasraj "Jasraj"), [Kala Ramnath](/wiki/Kala_Ramnath "Kala Ramnath"), [Sanjeev Abhyankar](/wiki/Sanjeev_Abhyankar "Sanjeev Abhyankar") | Emphasis on melody, known for bhajans, sapaat taans and gamak taans, use of sargam |
| [Sham Chaurasia gharana](/wiki/Sham_Chaurasia_gharana "Sham Chaurasia gharana") | Miyan Chand Khan, Miyan Suraj Khan | Late 16th century | [Salamat Ali and Nazakat Ali Khan](/wiki/Sham_Chaurasia_gharana "Sham Chaurasia gharana") | Emphasis on layakari using bol-taan and tihai, fast sargam and taan patterns |
| Kunwar Shyam gharana | Goswami Lalji Maharaj ("Kunwar Shyam")[[8]](#cite_note-8) | Late 19th century | Laxman Prasad Jaipurwale, Govind Prasad Jaipurwale, Bhavdeep Jaipurwale, [Amit Chaudhuri](/wiki/Amit_Chaudhuri "Amit Chaudhuri")[[9]](#cite_note-9) | Meend-based alap, intracate taan patterns and laykari[[10]](#cite_note-10) |
| [Panchakshara gharana](/wiki/Puttaraj_Gawai "Puttaraj Gawai") | [Panchakshara Gawai](/wiki/Panchakshara_Gawai "Panchakshara Gawai"), [Puttaraj Gawai](/wiki/Puttaraj_Gawai "Puttaraj Gawai") | Late 19th century | [Panchakshara Gawai](/wiki/Panchakshara_Gawai "Panchakshara Gawai"), [Puttaraj Gawai](/wiki/Puttaraj_Gawai "Puttaraj Gawai"), [Basavaraj Rajguru](/wiki/Basavaraj_Rajguru "Basavaraj Rajguru"), Arjun Sa Nakod, Chandrashekhar Puranik Math, Sheshadri Gawai, Siddaram Korwar, Panchakshari Swamimati Katti, [Venkatesh Kumar](/wiki/Venkatesh_Kumar "Venkatesh Kumar"), many more | Puttaraj Gawai conducted a spiritual and artistic study of the Panchakshara Swara Lipi and musicology, and composed new kritis and bandishas based on it. In the books they authored—*Hindustani Gaanasudha Part 1* and *Hindustani Gaanasudha Part 2*—they hand-wrote notations for 66 ragas in Braille script.  The gharana has one of the longest lineages of disciples, following traditional guru–shishya parampara.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |

### Dhrupad gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dhrupad gharanas")]

The [dhrupad](/wiki/Dhrupad "Dhrupad") tradition includes four original styles:[[11]](#cite_note-11)

* [Dagarbani](/wiki/Dagar_gharana "Dagar gharana") ("sadharani geeti")
* Gauharbani gharana|Gauharbani
* Khandarbani gharana
* Nauharbani gharana

Today's surviving dhrupad traditions are descendants of the aforementioned four styles.

* [Dagar gharana](/wiki/Dagar_gharana "Dagar gharana"), founded by the Dagar family
* [Bishnupur gharana](/wiki/Bishnupur_gharana "Bishnupur gharana"), founded by [Kirtankars](/wiki/Kirtankar "Kirtankar") in [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal") (13th century)
* [Darbhanga gharana](/wiki/Darbhanga_gharana "Darbhanga gharana"), founded in [Darbhanga](/wiki/Darbhanga "Darbhanga"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar")
* Dumraon Gharana, Buxar, Bihar founded by Dumraon Maharaj, and musician Pandit Ghanarang Dubey
* Bettiah gharana, founded in [Bettiah](/wiki/Bettiah "Bettiah"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar")
* Talwandi gharana
* [Mewati gharana](/wiki/Mewati_gharana "Mewati gharana"), founded by Wahid Khan (Beenkar) and [Ghagge Nazir Khan](/wiki/Ghagge_Nazir_Khan "Ghagge Nazir Khan"). Descended from Khandarbani gharana.
* Kalpi gharana[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Thumri gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Thumri gharanas")]

In the Benares gharana, the words in the text of a song are musically embellished to bring out their meaning, while the Lucknow gharana presents intricately embellished and delicate [thumris](/wiki/Thumris "Thumris") that are explicit in their eroticism. The principal feature of the [thumri](/wiki/Thumri "Thumri") of the [Patiala gharana](/wiki/Patiala_gharana "Patiala gharana") is its incorporation of the [tappa](/wiki/Pashto_music#Tappa "Pashto music") from the Punjab region. It is with this [tappa](/wiki/Pashto_music#Tappa "Pashto music") element that the Patiala gharana makes its impact, departing from the [khyal](/wiki/Khyal "Khyal")-dominated Benares [thumris](/wiki/Thumris "Thumris") and the dance-oriented Lucknow [thumris](/wiki/Thumris "Thumris").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The Benares gharana was founded by [Kirtankars](/wiki/Kirtankar "Kirtankar") in the 13th century and revived by [Siddheshwari Devi](/wiki/Siddheshwari_Devi "Siddheshwari Devi"), [Rasoolan Bai](/wiki/Rasoolan_Bai "Rasoolan Bai"), Badi Moti Bai, Mahadev Mishra, [Girija Devi](/wiki/Girija_Devi "Girija Devi") (mid-20th century) and Savita Devi.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Instrumental gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Instrumental gharanas")]

See also: [Category:Instrumental gharanas](/wiki/Category:Instrumental_gharanas "Category:Instrumental gharanas")

### Tabla gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tabla gharanas")]

The following are the six widely accepted gharanas (ordered based on chronology of founding):[[4]](#cite_note-itc-4)

* [Delhi gharana](/wiki/Delhi_gharana "Delhi gharana") is the oldest of the tabla gharanas
* [Ajrara gharana](/wiki/Ajrara_gharana "Ajrara gharana") is an offshoot of and closely associated with the Delhi Gharana
* [Lucknow gharana](/wiki/Lucknow_gharana "Lucknow gharana") has rhythmic development through [Kathak](/wiki/Kathak "Kathak")
* [Benares gharana](/wiki/Benares_gharana "Benares gharana")
* [Punjab gharana](/wiki/Punjab_gharana "Punjab gharana"), popularized by [Alla Rakha](/wiki/Alla_Rakha "Alla Rakha") and [Zakir Husain](/wiki/Zakir_Hussain_(musician) "Zakir Hussain (musician)"), developed through its original [Pakhavaj](/wiki/Pakhavaj "Pakhavaj") repertoire
* [Farrukhabad gharana](/wiki/Farrukhabad_gharana "Farrukhabad gharana") is the youngest accepted tabla gharana, and an offshoot of all of the Gharanas, featuring their main concepts

| Gharana | Founding artists | Approximate founding date | Founding location | Famous exponents |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Delhi gharana](/wiki/Delhi_gharana "Delhi gharana") | Siddhar Khan | Early 18th century | [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi") | Ghami Khan, Imam Ali Khan, Munnu Khan, Latif Ahmed Khan, Shafaat Ahmed Khan |
| [Ajrara gharana](/wiki/Ajrara_gharana "Ajrara gharana") | Kallu Khan, Miru Khan | Early 19th century | [Ajrara](/wiki/Ajrara "Ajrara") | Habibuddin Khan, Mehboob Hussain Khan, Sudhirkumar Saxena, Manju Khan, Yusuf Khan, Ramjan Khan, Sarwar Sabri, Akram Khan |
| [Lucknow gharana](/wiki/Lucknow_gharana "Lucknow gharana") | Miyan Bakshu | 19th century | [Lucknow](/wiki/Lucknow "Lucknow") | Ilmas Hussain Khan, Timir Roy Chowdhury, Achchan Maharaj, Anil Bhattacharjee, Biswajit Bhattacharjee, Santosh Biswas, [Swapan Chaudhuri](/wiki/Swapan_Chaudhuri "Swapan Chaudhuri"), Faiyaz Khan |
| [Benares gharana](/wiki/Benares_gharana "Benares gharana") | Ram Sahai | Late 18th century | [Benares](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") | Ram Sahai, Kanthe Maharaj, [Anokhelal Mishra](/wiki/Anokhelal_Mishra "Anokhelal Mishra"), [Shamta Prasad](/wiki/Shamta_Prasad "Shamta Prasad"), [Kishen Maharaj](/wiki/Kishen_Maharaj "Kishen Maharaj"), Mahapurush Mishra, [Kumar Bose](/wiki/Kumar_Bose "Kumar Bose"), [Ananda Gopal Bandopadhyay](/wiki/Ananda_Gopal_Bandopadhyay "Ananda Gopal Bandopadhyay"), Samar Saha, [Sandeep Das](/wiki/Sandeep_Das "Sandeep Das") |
| [Farrukhabad gharana](/wiki/Farrukhabad_gharana "Farrukhabad gharana") | Haji Vilayat Ali Khan | 19th century | [Farrukhabad](/wiki/Farrukhabad "Farrukhabad") | Ustad Amir Hussain Khan, Masit Khan, Ahmedjan Thirakwa, [Jnan Prakash Ghosh](/wiki/Jnan_Prakash_Ghosh "Jnan Prakash Ghosh"), Keramatullah Khan, Kanai Dutta, Shyamal Bose, [Shankar Ghosh](/wiki/Shankar_Ghosh_(musician) "Shankar Ghosh (musician)"), [Anindo Chatterjee](/wiki/Anindo_Chatterjee "Anindo Chatterjee"), [Bickram Ghosh](/wiki/Bickram_Ghosh "Bickram Ghosh") |
| [Punjab gharana](/wiki/Punjab_gharana "Punjab gharana") | Miyan Qader Baksh | 19th century | [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") | Qadeer Buksh, Shaukat Hussein Khan, [Alla Rakha Khan](/wiki/Alla_Rakha_Khan "Alla Rakha Khan"), [Zakir Hussain](/wiki/Zakir_Hussain_(musician) "Zakir Hussain (musician)"), [Yogesh Samsi](/wiki/Yogesh_Samsi "Yogesh Samsi"), Abdul Sattar Tari Khan |

## Dance gharanas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gharana&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dance gharanas")]

Main article: [Kathak § Gharanas](/wiki/Kathak#Gharanas "Kathak")

In Kathak performers today generally draw their lineage from four major schools of Kathak: the Jaipur-Atrauli gharana, the Lucknow dance gharana, the Benares gharana (born in the courts of the [Kachwaha](/wiki/Kachwaha "Kachwaha") [Rajput](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") kings, the Nawab of Oudh, and Varanasi respectively.) and the Raigarh gharana (born in the court of Maharaja Chakradhar Singh of Raigarh.)

The Lucknow gharana remains the most popular throughout the country. However, in recent time the Atrauli-Jaipur gharana has caught up and today most performers throughout India perform techniques belonging to both styles. With amalgamation of the techniques and poses from other dance forms, the purity of the movements and gestures may be diluted or modified along with the contemporary trends. Raigarh gharana is famous for its own distinctive composition and thousands of followers.
